                                                                                ERRATUM
                                       The employment by occupation data which appeared in the March 1967 issue of this publi-
                                       cation (pp. 14-15 and pp. 39-41) were not correctly adjusted to exclude 14 and 15 year-
                                       olds. Similarly, the unemployment rates by occupation (appearing on pp. 28-31 of the same
                                       issue) contain a few e r r o r s . The seasonally adjusted data on employment "by occupation
                                       and unemployment rates by occupation which appear in the February-June issues (Tables
                                       A-29 and A-33) are also subject to revision.       The entire package of employment and
                                       unemployment data by occupation is now being examined; correct historical series will
                                       be published in the July 1967 issue.

                                                                                  NOTE

                                     Periodically, the Bureau adjusts the industry employment series to a recent benchmark to improve
                                their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect the hours and earnings series because employment
                                levels are used as weights. Data from April 1965 forward are subject to revision at the time of the
                                next benchmark adjustment.      Beginning with September 1966 and subsequent issues of Employment and
                                Earnings and Monthly Report on the Labor Force, national data in Sections B, C, and D are based on
                                March 1965 benchmarks. Comparable data for prior periods are published in Employment and Earnings
                                Statistics for the United States, 1909-66, BLS Bulletin 1312-4.

                                     For further information regarding benchmark adjustments and other aspects of the program, the user
                                is referred to the technical note at the back of this volume.

                   1
                       Quarterly data included in February, May, August, and November issues.

                                                                  Technical Note

                                                      Additional information concerning the preparation
                                                      of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                                      and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                                      survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                                      technical notes for each of these series, available
                                                      from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.

                                                                 Establishment Data
                   COLLECTION                                                       on Labor Turnover. These schedules are of the "shuttle"
                                                                                    type, with space for each month of the calendar year.
                     Payroll reports provide current information on wage            The schedule is returned to the respondent each month
                  and salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turn-           by the collecting agency so that the next month's data
                  over in nonfarm establishments, by industry and geo-              can be entered. This procedure assures maximum
                  graphic location.                                                 comparability and accuracy of reporting, since the
                                                                                    respondent can see the figures he has reported for
                                                                                    previous months.
                   Federal-State Cooperation
                     Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies,                The BLS 790 provides for entry of data on the num-
                  the respondent fills out only one employment or labor             ber of full- and part-time workers on the payrolls of non-
                  turnover schedule, which is then used for national, State,        agricultural establishments and, for most industries,
                  and area estimates. This eliminates duplicate reporting           payroll and man-hours of production and related workers
                  on the part of respondents and, together with the use of          or nonsupervisory workers for the pay period which
                  identical techniques at the national and State levels,            includes the 12th of the month. The labor turnover
                  insures maximum comparability of estimates.                       schedule provides for the collection of information on
                                                                                    the total number of accessions and separations, by type,
                      State agencies mail the forms to the establishments           during the calendar month.
                  and examine the returns for consistency, accuracy, and
                  completeness. The States use the information to prepare
                  State and area series and then send the data to the BLS           CONCEPTS
                  for use in preparing the national series.

                       Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring        directly to the corresponding unadjusted series, but
                   seasonal movement which can be estimated on the basis             seasonally adjusted employment totals for all employees
                   of past experience. By eliminating that part of the change        and production workers by industry divisions are ob-
                   which can be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is          tained by summing the seasonally adjusted data which
                   possible to observe the cyclical and other nonseasonal            are published for component industries. Indexes of
                   movements in the series. However, in evaluating devia-            aggregate weekly man-hours seasonally adjusted, for
                   tions from the seasonal pattern—that is, changes in a             mining, contract construction, and the major industries
                   seasonally adjusted series—it is important to note that           in manufacturing are obtained by multiplying average
                   seasonal adjustment is merely an approximation based              weekly hours, seasonally adjusted, by production workers,
                   on past experience. Seasonally adjusted estimates have            seasonally adjusted and dividing by the 1957-59 base.
                   a broader margin of possible error than the original              For total, manufacturing, and durable and nondurable
                   data on which they are based, since they are subject not          goods, the indexes of aggregate weekly man-hours,
                   only to sampling and other errors but, in addition, are           seasonally adjusted, are obtained by summing the aggre-
                   affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment          gate weekly man-hours, seasonally adjusted, for the
                   process itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected           appropriate component industries and dividing by the
                   labor force and establishment data are published regularly        1957-59 base.
                   in Employment and Earnings and Monthly Report on the
